<task_result>
# JavaScript/TypeScript Graph Database Solutions for Browser-Based Applications

Based on external search results, here are 3 viable graph database solutions for your Vite + Aurelia 2 frontend with client-side storage preferences:

## 1. GUN (Decentralized Graph Database)

**GitHub:** https://github.com/amark/gun

### Overview
GUN is a decentralized, real-time graph database that runs in browsers with built-in P2P synchronization. It uses IndexedDB/LocalStorage for client-side persistence and supports offline-first applications.

### Pros & Cons

| Pros                                                  | Cons                                             |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| ✅ Works entirely in browser (IndexedDB/localStorage) | ❌ Learning curve for decentralized concepts     |
| ✅ Real-time P2P synchronization built-in             | ❌ Smaller ecosystem vs traditional databases    |
| ✅ Offline-first with automatic sync                  | ❌ Complex queries require custom implementation |
| ✅ End-to-end encryption (SEA module)                 | ❌ Documentation can be sparse in areas          |
| ✅ 19k+ GitHub stars, active community                | ❌ Performance may vary with large datasets      |
| ✅ TypeScript support available                       | ❌ CRDT-based (conflict resolution may surprise) |
| ✅ Zero backend required for basic usage              | ❌ Not ideal for complex relational queries      |
| ✅ 20M+ API ops/sec, ~9KB gzipped                     |                                                  |
| ✅ Production-ready (Internet Archive, 100s of apps)  |                                                  |

**Client-Side Storage:** ✅ IndexedDB, LocalStorage, in-memory  
**Backend Integration:** Optional relay peers for sync  
**License:** MIT

---

## 2. LevelGraph (LevelDB-Based Graph Database)

**GitHub:** https://github.com/levelgraph/levelgraph

### Overview
LevelGraph is a graph database built on LevelDB that works in both Node.js and browsers. In browsers, it uses IndexedDB through Level.js. It follows the Hexastore approach with six indices for fast triple access.

### Pros & Cons

| Pros                                               | Cons                                        |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| ✅ Works in browser via IndexedDB (Level.js)       | ❌ Requires LevelDB knowledge               |
| ✅ 1.5k+ GitHub stars, mature project              | ❌ Less active development recently         |
| ✅ Hexastore approach (6 indices) for fast queries | ❌ RDF/triple-based model (learning curve)  |
| ✅ Stream-based API for large datasets             | ❌ Limited built-in relationship management |
| ✅ Navigator API for fluent graph traversal        | ❌ No built-in encryption/security          |
| ✅ Plugin ecosystem (JSON-LD, N3/Turtle support)   | ❌ Browser bundle size larger (~100KB+)     |
| ✅ Pattern matching & search capabilities          | ❌ Manual relationship maintenance          |
| ✅ Well-documented API                             | ❌ No real-time sync built-in               |

**Client-Side Storage:** ✅ IndexedDB (via Level.js)  
**Backend Integration:** Can sync with server-side LevelDB  
**License:** MIT

---

## 3. Fortune.js (Graph Database Abstraction Layer)

**GitHub:** https://github.com/fortunejs/fortune

### Overview
Fortune.js is a non-native graph database abstraction layer that implements graph features at the application level. It works in both Node.js and browsers, with IndexedDB for browser storage and automatic relationship management.

### Pros & Cons

| Pros                                           | Cons                                          |
| ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| ✅ Works in browser with IndexedDB by default  | ❌ Abstraction layer (not native graph DB)    |
| ✅ 1.5k+ GitHub stars                          | ❌ Performance overhead from abstraction      |
| ✅ Automatic inverse relationship updates      | ❌ Limited to predefined record types         |
| ✅ Referential integrity enforced              | ❌ Less flexible than native graph solutions  |
| ✅ Database-agnostic (swap adapters)           | ❌ Smaller community vs GUN                   |
| ✅ Type validations built-in                   | ❌ Requires schema definitions upfront        |
| ✅ HTTP/WebSocket listeners available          | ❌ No decentralized/P2P features              |
| ✅ Plain data objects (no ORM/active record)   | ❌ Less suitable for complex graph traversals |
| ✅ Works with MongoDB, Postgres, NeDB adapters |                                               |

**Client-Side Storage:** ✅ IndexedDB (browser), in-memory  
**Backend Integration:** HTTP/WS listeners, multiple DB adapters  
**License:** MIT

---

## Recommendation Matrix

| Criteria               | GUN        | LevelGraph | Fortune.js |
| ---------------------- | ---------- | ---------- | ---------- |
| **Ease of Setup**          | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐     | ⭐⭐⭐⭐   |
| **Client-Side Storage**    | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐   | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Performance**            | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐   | ⭐⭐⭐     |
| **Graph Query Complexity** | ⭐⭐⭐     | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐     |
| **Real-Time Sync**         | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐       | ⭐⭐⭐⭐   |
| **.NET Integration**       | ⭐⭐⭐     | ⭐⭐⭐     | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Community/Support**      | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐     | ⭐⭐⭐     |

## Integration with Your Tech Stack

### For Vite + Aurelia 2:
- **GUN:** Best for real-time collaborative features, offline-first apps
- **LevelGraph:** Best for complex graph queries, RDF/semantic data
- **Fortune.js:** Best for structured data with relationships, CRUD operations

### For .NET 9 WebAPI Backend:
- **GUN:** Can sync with relay peers (Node.js), limited .NET integration
- **LevelGraph:** Can sync LevelDB data, requires custom .NET implementation
- **Fortune.js:** HTTP listener can integrate with .NET API via REST/GraphQL

## Final Recommendation

For your use case (Vite + Aurelia 2 frontend with preference for client-side storage):

1. **GUN** - If you need real-time sync, offline support, and don't mind decentralized architecture
2. **LevelGraph** - If you need powerful graph queries and are comfortable with RDF/triple stores
3. **Fortune.js** - If you want simple relationship management with potential backend sync

All three are real, active projects with working GitHub repositories and browser support.

<task_result>
Based on my external research, here are 3 real JavaScript/TypeScript graph database solutions for browser-based applications:

## 1. GUN.js - Decentralized Graph Database

**GitHub:** https://github.com/amark/gun  
**Stars:** 19k+ | **Active since:** 2014

### Overview
GUN is a decentralized, real-time graph database that runs in browsers and Node.js. It's a cybersecurity protocol for syncing decentralized graph data with built-in encryption and offline-first capabilities.

### Key Features
- **Native graph database** with key/value, tables, documents support
- **Client-side storage**: IndexedDB, LocalStorage, in-memory
- **P2P synchronization** with CRDT-based conflict resolution
- **End-to-end encryption** (SEA - Security, Encryption, Authorization)
- **Real-time by default** with reactive queries
- **Offline-first** architecture
- **~9KB gzipped** minimal footprint

### Pros/Cons Table

| Pros                                           | Cons                                          |
| ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| ✅ True graph database structure               | ❌ Steeper learning curve for graph concepts  |
| ✅ Decentralized P2P sync (no server required) | ❌ Less mature ecosystem than alternatives    |
| ✅ Built-in encryption & security              | ❌ Complex debugging for distributed issues   |
| ✅ Works offline by default                    | ❌ Smaller community compared to PouchDB/RxDB |
| ✅ Extremely lightweight (~9KB)                | ❌ Limited querying capabilities vs SQL       |
| ✅ Real-time synchronization built-in          | ❌ Documentation can be sparse in places      |
| ✅ Multiplayer/realtime apps made easy         | ❌ Performance tuning requires expertise      |
| ✅ TypeScript support available                |                                               |

### Tech Stack Compatibility
- **Vite + Aurelia 2**: ✅ Direct browser integration
- **.NET 9 WebAPI**: ✅ Can sync with .NET via WebSocket/HTTP
- **Client-side storage**: ✅ IndexedDB, LocalStorage, memory

---

## 2. RxDB - Reactive Database with Graph-like Features

**GitHub:** https://github.com/pubkey/rxdb  
**Stars:** 23.1k+ | **Active since:** 2018

### Overview
RxDB (Reactive Database) is a local-first, NoSQL database for JavaScript applications. While not a pure graph database, it supports graph-like data structures through document collections with relationships.

### Key Features
- **Multiple storage backends**: IndexedDB, LocalStorage, OPFS, SQLite, in-memory
- **Reactive queries** with Observable subscriptions
- **Real-time sync** with custom backends (GraphQL, CouchDB, Supabase, Firestore, etc.)
- **Conflict resolution** with CRDT support
- **Schema validation** and migrations
- **Encryption** support
- **TypeScript-first** design
- **23+ storage adapters** available

### Pros/Cons Table

| Pros                                      | Cons                                     |
| ----------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| ✅ Excellent TypeScript support           | ❌ Not a native graph database           |
| ✅ Flexible storage layer (swap backends) | ❌ Requires modeling graph as documents  |
| ✅ Reactive/observable queries            | ❌ Complex join queries not native       |
| ✅ Mature ecosystem (23k+ stars)          | ❌ Larger bundle size (~50KB+)           |
| ✅ Multiple replication protocols         | ❌ Premium features require paid license |
| ✅ Great documentation                    | ❌ Eventual consistency learning curve   |
| ✅ Works with any backend infrastructure  | ❌ Client-side complexity increases      |
| ✅ Battle-tested in production            |                                          |

### Tech Stack Compatibility
- **Vite + Aurelia 2**: ✅ Excellent browser support
- **.NET 9 WebAPI**: ✅ Custom replication endpoints with .NET
- **Client-side storage**: ✅ IndexedDB, LocalStorage, OPFS, memory

---

## 3. Dexie.js - Modern IndexedDB Wrapper with Relationship Support

**GitHub:** https://github.com/dexie/Dexie.js  
**Stars:** 14.1k+ | **Active since:** 2014

### Overview
Dexie.js is a minimalistic wrapper for IndexedDB with a MongoDB-like query API. While primarily document-oriented, it supports graph-like structures through relationships and has excellent TypeScript support.

### Key Features
- **IndexedDB abstraction** with friendly API
- **Relationship support** through foreign keys
- **Live queries** with reactive updates
- **TypeScript-first** with full type safety
- **Bulk operations** for performance
- **Sync capabilities** via Dexie Cloud (paid) or custom
- **Cross-tab synchronization**
- **14+ years** of active development

### Pros/Cons Table

| Pros                                   | Cons                                                 |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| ✅ Excellent TypeScript support        | ❌ Not a graph database (document store)             |
| ✅ Simple, intuitive API               | ❌ Must manually implement graph traversal           |
| ✅ Built on stable IndexedDB standard  | ❌ No built-in sync (requires Dexie Cloud or custom) |
| ✅ Great performance (bulk operations) | ❌ Limited query capabilities vs true graph DB       |
| ✅ Small bundle size (~20KB)           | ❌ Dexie Cloud is paid service                       |
| ✅ Extensive documentation & examples  | ❌ Complex relationships need manual handling        |
| ✅ 14+ years of production use         | ❌ No native conflict resolution                     |
| ✅ Works in all modern browsers        |                                                      |

### Tech Stack Compatibility
- **Vite + Aurelia 2**: ✅ Direct browser integration
- **.NET 9 WebAPI**: ✅ Custom sync via .NET backend
- **Client-side storage**: ✅ IndexedDB only (most reliable browser DB)

---

## Additional Options (Not Recommended)

### PouchDB
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/apache/pouchdb (17.6k stars)
- **Note**: Document database (CouchDB-compatible), not graph-focused
- **Use if**: You need CouchDB sync ecosystem

### LokiJS
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/techfort/LokiJS (6.8k stars)
- **Note**: In-memory document database, not actively maintained
- **Use if**: You need fast in-memory operations

### ForerunnerDB
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/Irrelon/ForerunnerDB (719 stars)
- **Note**: Document database with MongoDB-like queries, small community
- **Use if**: You need MongoDB query syntax in browser

---

## Recommendation for Your Use Case

**Best choice: GUN.js** - If you need true graph database features with decentralized sync.

**Alternative: RxDB** - If you prioritize TypeScript support, maturity, and can model graph as documents.

**Simple option: Dexie.js** - If you want the simplest API and can implement graph logic yourself.

All three support your tech stack (Vite + Aurelia 2 frontend, .NET 9 backend) and work with client-side storage (IndexedDB/LocalStorage).

<task_result>
Based on my research, here are **3 real JavaScript/TypeScript graph database solutions** for browser-based applications:

---

## 1. **LevelGraph**
**GitHub:** https://github.com/levelgraph/levelgraph

| **Aspect**          | **Details**                                |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| **Type**            | Graph database built on LevelDB        |
| **Browser Support** | ✅ Yes - Uses IndexedDB in browser     |
| **Language**        | JavaScript                             |
| **Stars**           | 1.5k                                   |
| **Storage**         | IndexedDB (browser), LevelDB (Node.js) |

### Pros:
- Works in both Node.js and browser with same API
- Uses IndexedDB for client-side storage in browsers
- Built on proven LevelDB technology
- Supports triple pattern matching and graph traversal
- Has Hexastore approach with 6 indices for fast triple access
- Supports RDF/JSON-LD extensions
- Active community with 1.5k+ stars
- Supports filtering, pagination, and streams

### Cons:
- Not actively maintained (last commit in 2024)
- Learning curve for Hexastore concepts
- No built-in visualization
- Limited query planning capabilities
- Requires understanding of triple-based data model
- Not as feature-rich as server-side graph databases

---

## 2. **GUN (gun.js)**
**GitHub:** https://github.com/amark/gun

| **Aspect**          | **Details**                                      |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| **Type**            | Decentralized graph database                 |
| **Browser Support** | ✅ Yes - Runs entirely in browser            |
| **Language**        | JavaScript                                   |
| **Stars**           | 19k                                          |
| **Storage**         | LocalStorage, IndexedDB, RAD (Radix storage) |

### Pros:
- Decentralized P2P architecture with real-time sync
- Works offline-first with automatic conflict resolution (CRDT)
- End-to-end encryption built-in (SEA module)
- Extremely lightweight (~9KB gzipped)
- Multiplayer/real-time by default
- Supports key/value, documents, tables, and graph data
- Used in production by Internet Archive and 100s of apps
- No server required - fully client-side
- Excellent community support (19k+ stars)
- Supports WebRTC for browser-to-browser communication

### Cons:
- Steep learning curve for decentralized concepts
- Performance may degrade with very large graphs
- Limited querying capabilities compared to SQL databases
- Documentation can be overwhelming
- Requires understanding of CRDT and distributed systems
- Not ideal for traditional CRUD applications
- Sync behavior can be unpredictable without proper configuration

---

## 3. **TypeGraph**
**GitHub:** https://github.com/nicia-ai/typegraph

| **Aspect**          | **Details**                                    |
| --------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| **Type**            | TypeScript knowledge graph library         |
| **Browser Support** | ✅ Yes - Works with SQLite/PostgreSQL      |
| **Language**        | TypeScript                                 |
| **Stars**           | 15                                         |
| **Storage**         | SQLite, PostgreSQL, D1, libsql, bun:sqlite |

### Pros:
- TypeScript-first with compile-time type safety
- Uses Zod schemas for validation
- Fluent query builder API
- Supports rich semantics (subClassOf, implies, inverseOf)
- Works with existing SQL databases (no separate graph service)
- Can start with SQLite and migrate to PostgreSQL
- Designed for knowledge graphs and RAG context
- Edge/serverless compatible
- Excellent for identity/permissions and relationship-heavy models
- Modern API with async/await support

### Cons:
- Very new project (only 15 stars)
- Not as battle-tested as other solutions
- Requires SQL database (not pure client-side storage)
- Limited community support
- No built-in P2P/decentralized features
- Requires understanding of both SQL and graph concepts
- Not suitable for billion-scale graphs

---

## **Recommendation Summary**

For your **Vite + Aurelia 2** frontend with **.NET 9 WebAPI** backend:

### **Best for Client-Side Storage:**
- **GUN (gun.js)** - If you need fully decentralized, offline-first with real-time sync
- **LevelGraph** - If you need traditional graph database with IndexedDB storage

### **Best for SQL Integration:**
- **TypeGraph** - If you want to keep graph data in your existing SQL infrastructure

### **Tech Stack Compatibility:**
- All three work well with Vite + TypeScript
- GUN and LevelGraph are pure client-side (no backend needed)
- TypeGraph requires SQL backend but integrates with .NET WebAPI easily

### **Production Readiness:**
- **GUN** - Most mature (19k stars, used by Internet Archive)
- **LevelGraph** - Moderately mature (1.5k stars, stable but less active)
- **TypeGraph** - Newest (15 stars, modern but less proven)
